title: How Mariner 4 Rewrote What We Know About Mars
author: Jake Currie
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementAstronomyHow Mariner 4 Rewrote What We Know About MarsIt killed the dreams of science-fiction fans, but kick-started decades of Martian explorationJCBy Jake Currie5:00 PM CDT on July 13, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskySixty-one years ago this week, humanity caught our first glimpses of another planet from up close as Mariner 4 made its closest pass by Mars. During the missions, NASA engineers waited with bated breath. Mariner 3, the first mission to see the red planet, had been sidelined by equipment failures, and drifted powerlessly into space when its batteries died. As Mariner 4 made its final approach, unusual readings from its data recorder (a spare not originally intended to be used onboard) were making NASA engineers nervous.Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Astronomers Discover Ceres’s Crust is Actually 90% WaterShareNextStayWhen the spacecraft finally started transmitting (which took up to six hours for a single photo), engineers at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory were anxious to find out if everything was working properly. So they printed out the raw numerical data from the onboard camera on 3-inch strips of ticker tape and assembled them on a wall. Using pastel crayons purchased from an art store, they started shading the numbers according to the brightness of each pixel, like a giant paint-by-number painting. Read more: “Mars Is a Second-Rate Backup Plan”Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisement
